title: Enforcement of Arbitral Awards
content:
Session 5: Enforcement of Arbitral Awards 
 
Shivhare v. ED [2010 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The appellant, Shivhare, along with another individual, was issued a show cause notice under s3(c) of FEMA.  
‚û¢‚Äã Allegation = he had received unauthorised payments amounting to approximately ‚Çπ5 crores under instructions 
from PROI ‚Üí allegedly connected with illegal cricket betting operations conducted by the appellant. 
‚û¢‚Äã During a search of the appellant‚Äôs residence, an amount of ‚Çπ1 lakh was confiscated.  
‚ùñ‚Äã The appellant was further called upon to explain why this amount should not be credited to the CG under s13(2) of FEMA, 
which deals with confiscation following contraventions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã After adjudication, a penalty of ‚Çπ2 crores was imposed on the appellant.  
‚û¢‚Äã Aggrieved by this decision, the appellant preferred an appeal before the Appellate Tribunal for Foreign Exchange 
under s19(2) of FEMA ‚Üí dismissed the appeal and affirmed the penalty. 
189 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Instead of filing a statutory appeal to the HC under Section 35 of FEMA, the appellant filed a writ petition under Article 
226 of the Constitution before the HC, challenging the order of the Appellate Tribunal ‚Üí dismissed the writ petition, not 
on the ground of maintainability, but on the basis of lack of territorial jurisdiction. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The appellant then approached the SC, challenging the High Court‚Äôs dismissal and contending that a writ petition against 
the Appellate Tribunal‚Äôs order was maintainable. 
 
Issues: Whether a writ petition under Article 226 is maintainable against an order of the Appellate Tribunal for Foreign Exchange, in 
light of the statutory appellate remedy provided under Section 35 of FEMA. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 35, FEMA ‚Üí any person aggrieved by an order of the Appellate Tribunal may file an appeal to the HC on a 
question of law. 
‚ùñ‚Äã General Principle ‚Üí when a statutory forum for redressal of grievances is created by law, particularly in a fiscal statute, 
courts should ordinarily not entertain writ petitions bypassing the statutory remedy, especially when that remedy is 
efficacious. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA is a complete code, particularly in matters of adjudication and appeals.  
‚û¢‚Äã Once the Appellate Tribunal has passed an order, the statute expressly provides for a further appeal to the High 
Court under Section 35, albeit limited to questions of law. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Where the legislature has consciously created a specific appellate route, litigants cannot be permitted to bypass statutory 
conditions attached to that route by invoking writ jurisdiction ‚Üí conditions may include: 
‚û¢‚Äã limitation periods, 
‚û¢‚Äã payment of court fees, 
‚û¢‚Äã pre-deposit of penalty amounts, or 
‚û¢‚Äã other procedural requirements. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Allowing a writ petition in such circumstances would enable litigants to defeat the legislative intent behind s35 ‚Üí 
particularly significant in the context of fiscal statutes, where regulatory discipline and procedural compliance are central. 
‚ùñ‚Äã While the HC, in this case, had dismissed the writ petition on the ground of territorial jurisdiction ‚Üí ought not to have 
entertained the writ petition at all, given the availability of an efficacious statutory remedy ‚Üí would encourage a trend of 
forum shopping and statutory bypassing, which writ courts should actively discourage. 
‚ùñ‚Äã While writ jurisdiction is not completely barred, it should be exercised only in exceptional circumstances, such as: 
‚û¢‚Äã violation of principles of natural justice, 
‚û¢‚Äã lack of jurisdiction, or 
‚û¢‚Äã challenge to the vires of the statute. 
‚ùñ‚Äã None of these exceptional circumstances were present in the appellant‚Äôs case. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã A writ petition is not ordinarily maintainable against an order of the Appellate Tribunal for Foreign Exchange. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The proper remedy against such an order is a statutory appeal to the High Court under Section 35 of FEMA. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Entertaining writ petitions in the face of an efficacious statutory remedy would undermine the statutory framework and 
legislative intent. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Accordingly, dismissed the appeal, though for reasons different from those given by the HC, and upheld the principle that 
statutory appellate remedies under FEMA must be exhausted. 
 
Cruz City Holdings v. Unitech Ltd. [2017 | DHC | 1 J] 
Facts: 
190 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã
 
‚ùñ‚Äã The investment was governed by a SHA and a Keepwell Agreement.  
‚û¢‚Äã Under the SHA, Cruz City was granted a put option allowing it to exit the investment at a 15% post-tax IRR if 
construction of the project did not commence within the stipulated period, with Arsanovia and Burley being 
obligated to purchase its shares.  
‚û¢‚Äã Under the Keepwell Agreement, Unitech undertook to ensure that Burley met its payment obligations and to 
provide funds if Burley was unable to do so. 
‚ùñ‚Äã When construction was delayed, Cruz City exercised the put option and invoked the agreement, leading to LCIA 
arbitration and an award in its favour.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Cruz City then sought enforcement of the foreign award in India, which Unitech opposed under s48 of the Arbitration and 
Conciliation Act, 1996, contending that enforcement would violate FEMA and RBI regulations and was therefore contrary 
to Indian public policy.  
 
Issue: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether enforcement of the foreign arbitral award would violate FEMA and regulations framed thereunder. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the obligations imposed on Unitech under the Keepwell Agreement amounted to an illegal corporate guarantee. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the award directed an impermissible overseas investment without compliance with valuation norms. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the contractual structure provided an assured return, prohibited under FEMA and RBI circulars. 
‚ùñ‚Äã If FEMA violations existed, whether such violations bar enforcement of a foreign arbitral award under Section 48. 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Foreign Exchange Management (Guarantees) Regulations, 2000 
‚û¢‚Äã Regulation 3: Prohibits Indian residents from giving guarantees or sureties in respect of liabilities owed by another 
Indian resident to a non-resident. 
‚û¢‚Äã Regulation 5(b)(i): Permits guarantees relating to obligations having a direct business connection with the 
guarantor‚Äôs business. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Foreign Exchange Management (Transfer or Issue of Any Foreign Security) Regulations, 2004 
‚û¢‚Äã Regulation 2(e): Defines overseas direct investment. 
‚û¢‚Äã Regulation 5: Permits overseas investment by certain Indian entities. 
‚û¢‚Äã Regulation 6(6): Requires valuation of shares by a Category-I Merchant Banker or foreign investment banker for 
overseas acquisitions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Foreign Exchange Management (Permissible Capital Account Transactions) Regulations, 2000 
‚û¢‚Äã Regulation 3(1): Permits specified capital account transactions subject to RBI regulations. 
‚û¢‚Äã Regulation 3(2): Requires compliance with valuation norms and approvals. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA NDI Rules (Assured Returns) 
‚û¢‚Äã Guiding principle: No assured exit price for foreign investors at the time of investment. 
‚û¢‚Äã Rule 9(5): Optionality clauses permitted only without assured returns, subject to pricing guidelines and lock-in 
period. 
‚û¢‚Äã Definition of equity instruments under Rule 2(k). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 48, Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 
191 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Enforcement of foreign awards may be refused only on limited grounds, including violation of public policy of 
India. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Unitech advanced four principal FEMA-based challenges to resist enforcement of the award. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Alleged illegal overseas investment ‚Üí enforcement of the award required it to purchase shares of Kerrush, which amounted 
to an overseas investment violating the 2004 Regulations, particularly due to non-compliance with valuation requirements. 
‚û¢‚Äã REJECTED ‚Üí the award did not direct a fresh investment, but merely enforced a pre-existing contractual 
obligation ‚Üí payment pursuant to an arbitral award could not be equated with a voluntary overseas investment 
decision. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Keepwell Agreement as an illegal guarantee ‚Üí Unitech contended that its obligations were in substance a corporate 
guarantee for Burley‚Äôs liabilities, prohibited under Regulation 3 of the Guarantees Regulations, 2000, which had no genuine 
business, thereby excluding the application of Regulation 5(b) ‚Äì REJECTED 
‚û¢‚Äã First, the contention directly contradicted Unitech‚Äôs own representations in the Keepwell Agreement, which 
affirmed that the agreement did not violate applicable law ‚Üí having contractually asserted legality, Unitech could 
not later resile from that position. 
‚û¢‚Äã Second, the issue was never raised before the arbitral tribunal ‚Üí constituted an afterthought and abuse of process. 
‚û¢‚Äã Third, even assuming Burley lacked bona fide business, Unitech could not escape contractual liability.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Any FEMA violation would expose Unitech to regulatory consequences, but could not be used as a shield 
to defeat enforcement of a valid arbitral award.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
From a public policy perspective, allowing Unitech to evade liability would be more destructive to the rule 
of law than enforcing the award. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Valuation and capital account transaction objections ‚Üí payment under the award constituted a capital account transaction 
requiring valuation and RBI approval ‚Äì REJECTED 
‚û¢‚Äã Such regulatory compliance issues could be addressed at the remittance stage, not at the stage of enforcement of the 
award ‚Üí could not be stalled merely due to possible downstream FEMA compliances. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Assured return argument ‚Üí the SHA and Keepwell Agreement effectively guaranteed Cruz City an assured return of 15% 
IRR, prohibited under FEMA and RBI circulars on FDI ‚Üí REJECTED 
‚û¢‚Äã The Put Option was not unconditional or open-ended ‚Üí contingent upon failure to commence construction 
within a specified time of 180 days ‚Üí Cruz City did not have an assured exit at the time of investment.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The arrangement was fundamentally risk-linked, not return-guaranteed. 
‚û¢‚Äã RBI circulars prohibiting assured returns were aimed at equity instruments disguised as debt, not contractual 
remedies arising from breach of representations.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Even if the structure incidentally resulted in a return, it arose due to breach of contractual assurances, not 
as an assured investment return. 
‚û¢‚Äã Even assuming that the arrangement violated FEMA, Unitech could not avoid liability.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Cruz City invested relying on Unitech‚Äôs representations ‚Üí if those representations were false or illegal, 
Unitech must bear both regulatory consequences and contractual liability. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The arbitral award does not violate FEMA. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Put Option did not constitute an assured return, as it was conditional, time-bound, and contingent on project delay. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Keepwell Agreement did not bar enforcement even if FEMA issues arose. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA violations, if any, may attract regulatory penalties, but do not render the award unenforceable. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Permitting Unitech to evade payment would undermine the rule of law more severely than enforcing the award. 
 
NTT Docomo Inc. v. Tata Sons Ltd. [2017 | DHC | 1 J] 
Facts: 
192 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã NTT Docomo Inc., a Japanese company, entered into a SHA with Tata Sons Ltd. and TTSL, making a strategic foreign 
direct investment in TTSL under this arrangement. 
‚û¢‚Äã Under Clause 5.7.2, if TTSL failed to satisfy certain Second Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) specified in the 
SHA, Tata was obligated to find a buyer or buyers for Docomo‚Äôs shares in TTSL at the ‚ÄúSale Price‚Äù, defined as the 
higher of: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
the fair value of the shares as of 31 March 2014 [which could require special permission from the RBI], or 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
50% of the price at which Docomo had originally purchased the shares. 
‚ùñ‚Äã TTSL failed to meet the Second KPIs ‚Üí Docomo issued a Sale Notice, invoking Clause 5.7.2 and calling upon Tata to find a 
buyer within the contractually defined Sale Period, which ended on 3 December 2014. Tata failed to do so. 
‚û¢‚Äã Due to failure of amicable resolution, the dispute was referred to arbitration ‚Üí Tata held liable and awarded 
Docomo a monetary sum with interest. 
‚ùñ‚Äã However, instead of straightforward enforcement, complications arose due to RBI‚Äôs intervention.  
‚û¢‚Äã Tata and Docomo placed Consent Terms before the DHC at the execution stage. RBI sought to intervene, arguing 
that enforcement of the award violated FEMA and NDI pricing guidelines. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the RBI had locus standi to intervene in enforcement proceedings of an arbitral award to which it was not a party. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Clause 5.7.2 of the SHA amounted to an assured return, prohibited under FEMA and NDI Guidelines. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether enforcement of the arbitral award violated FEMA, particularly pricing and transfer restrictions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the SHA or the Consent Terms were void or unenforceable under Indian law or public policy. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether payment pursuant to the award was damages or an impermissible share transfer consideration. 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA, 1999 s6(3): Empowers RBI to prohibit, restrict, or regulate transfer of securities by non-residents. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA (Transfer or Issue of Foreign Security) Regulations, 2000 
‚û¢‚Äã Regulation 9: Share transfers must adhere to pricing guidelines based on internationally accepted valuation 
methods. 
‚û¢‚Äã Regulation 9(2)(i): Permits transfer of shares from one non-resident to another non-resident at any price. 
‚ùñ‚Äã NDI Guidelines prohibit assured returns on equity investments. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 s48: Limited grounds for resisting enforcement of foreign awards. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã RBI‚Äôs locus standi 
‚û¢‚Äã RBI argued that since the arbitral award discussed FEMA provisions extensively and reached conclusions on their 
applicability, RBI‚Äôs presence was necessary at the execution stage ‚Äì REJECTED 
‚û¢‚Äã Private arbitral awards frequently interpret statutes and regulations, but this does not confer a right on the 
statutory regulator to intervene ‚Üí no provision under the Arbitration Act or CPC mandating RBI‚Äôs participation 
in such proceedings. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Permitting RBI to intervene would effectively convert every enforcement proceeding involving FEMA 
into a regulatory adjudication, which the law does not contemplate. 
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, RBI lacked a locus standi to intervene. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Nature of Clause 5.7.2 ‚Äì assured return or downside protection 
‚û¢‚Äã The clause was intended to protect Docomo from losing more than 50% of its investment, not to guarantee profits 
‚Üí functioned as downside protection, rather than an assured return. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
not an issuance or transfer of a ‚Äúsecurity‚Äù under Regulations 4 and 5 of FEMA 20; 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
merely a contractual obligation undertaken by Tata to find a buyer. 
‚û¢‚Äã Crucially, this obligation was always legally capable of performance.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Tata could have fulfilled it by locating a non-resident buyer, which is expressly permitted under 
Regulation 9(2)(i) at any price, including above fair market value. 
193 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Thus, the clause did not guarantee an exit at a fixed return at the time of investment, and therefore did 
not violate FEMA or NDI rules. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Damages versus transfer of shares 
‚û¢‚Äã What the arbitral award granted to Docomo was damages, not the price of shares.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The direction requiring return of share scrips to Tata was incidental + Docomo itself had no interest in 
retaining the shares. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
RBI‚Äôs objection was based on a mischaracterisation of the award, conflating damages payable for breach of 
contract with consideration for transfer of shares. 
‚û¢‚Äã RBI could not re-characterise the nature of payment under an arbitral award, particularly when Tata, the only 
party capable of opposing enforcement, had consented. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA and NDI rules are not blanket prohibitions 
‚û¢‚Äã FEMA and NDI guidelines are regulatory in nature, not absolute prohibitions + even where restrictions exist, 
FEMA envisages rectification through RBI permission. 
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, even assuming that some FEMA infraction arose, that would not render the SHA void, invalidate the 
arbitral award, or bar its enforcement. 
‚û¢‚Äã At most, it could expose parties to regulatory consequences, not defeat contractual or arbitral obligations. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Validity of SHA and Consent Terms 
‚û¢‚Äã SHA was neither void nor opposed to Indian law ‚Üí FEMA contains no absolute bar on contractual obligations, 
and Clause 5.7.2 was always legally performable using general permissions under FEMA. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Consent Terms placed before the Court were also upheld as valid ‚Üí  parties are free to settle disputes even at 
the execution stage of an award or decree. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Also considered broader public policy considerations, weighing strongly in favour of enforcement of the award: 
‚û¢‚Äã India‚Äôs reputation as an arbitration-friendly jurisdiction; 
‚û¢‚Äã the impact on foreign investment inflows; 
‚û¢‚Äã the importance of Indian entities honouring contractual commitments. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 56 (impossibility) argument 
‚û¢‚Äã Tata argued that complying with the award would result in FEMA violation and hence constituted an impossible 
act under Section 56 of the Contract Act ‚Üí a contract does not become impossible merely because compliance may 
require regulatory permission ‚Üí FEMA compliance mechanisms negate any claim of legal impossibility. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã RBI has no locus standi to intervene in enforcement proceedings of an arbitral award to which it is not a party. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Clause 5.7.2 of the SHA constituted downside protection, not an assured return. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The arbitral award granted damages, not share transfer consideration. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA and NDI guidelines are not blanket prohibitions and do not bar enforcement. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The SHA and Consent Terms were valid and enforceable. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Enforcement of the award was consistent with the public policy of India.
